risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To promote comparability of the intervention and comparison clusters with respect to cluster characteristics, practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners." Comment: The study describes a structured random allocation process using stratification and block randomisation with random block sizes, carried out by an independent statistician. These details support that a truly random sequence was generated.

Quote: "Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown." Comment: This explicitly states that recruiters enrolled women before cluster allocation was revealed, so the upcoming assignment could not be foreseen at enrolment. The paper also notes that randomisation was performed by "a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners," which supports separation of allocation from recruitment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control... women are not blinded in that they need to be aware that they may (intervention group) or may not (comparison group) be invited by the GP to discuss relationship issues as part of weave." Comment: Participants and personnel were explicitly unblinded. The study outcomes are largely subjective and plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation, including quality of life, mental health status, safety planning/behaviours, readiness for change, comfort discussing abuse, anxiety/depression, and other self-reported measures. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "There is no blinding as regards data collection based on the CONSORT guidelines [50], as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation)." Comment: Outcomes were assessed mainly by patient self-report and GP self-report. The study also states that "women are not blinded" and GPs cannot be blinded. Reported outcomes include multiple subjective self-reported measures such as quality of life (WHOQoL-Bref), mental health status (SF-12), safety behaviours, readiness for change, comfort discussing abuse, anxiety/depression, and GP readiness/attitudes. Because the assessors for these outcomes were the unblinded participants/clinicians themselves, and these measurements are highly susceptible to influence by knowledge of allocation, the domain is High risk under the worst-outcome rule.

Summary: This article is a study protocol/design paper and does not report observed post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes. It gives only anticipated losses: "Following randomisation, approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months based on data from the diamond cohort [51] and MOSAIC [52] leaving 89 women per group at 12 months." It also states planned outcome collection at "baseline, and at 6 and 12 months" and that analysis "will be intention-to-treat," but provides no actual per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers, no observed reasons for loss, and no outcome-specific denominators at follow-up. Comment: Because only expected attrition is reported and actual post-randomization missing outcome data cannot be calculated, the risk of attrition bias for the outcomes of interest is unclear rather than high.
